United Maritime Corp. data.title: Skillsoft Earnings Call: AI Pivot Lifts Margins
data.description:
Skillsoft Corp ((SKIL)) has held its Q4 earnings call. Read on for the main highlights of the call.

Easter Sale - 70% Off TipRanks

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Discover top-performing stock ideas and upgrade to a portfolio of market leaders with Smart Investor Picks

Skillsoft’s latest earnings call painted a cautiously optimistic picture, with management stressing tangible progress on its AI-led transformation, tighter cost control and stronger cash generation. While revenue slipped and GAAP losses widened on impairments and restructuring, the tone leaned more constructive as margin gains and surging AI engagement suggested the turnaround is taking hold despite macro and geopolitical headwinds.

AI-Native Pivot Recasts Skillsoft’s Core Business

Skillsoft declared its strategic overhaul largely complete, repositioning itself as an AI-native skills platform built around content, platform and data. A centerpiece is the new AI-native Percipio platform, unveiled in September and moving to general availability in February, which management views as the foundation for future growth and competitive differentiation.

Cost Cuts Fuel Targeted Reinvestment

The company removed roughly $45 million of gross costs, then deliberately recycled about half into go-to-market resources and AI product innovation. Executives emphasized that this reflects a more disciplined capital allocation approach, trimming legacy spending while still funding the areas they see as highest return in the new strategy.

New Platform Shows Early Customer Traction

On the product side, Skillsoft advanced its roadmap, including upgrades to its CAISY AI capabilities and the broad rollout of the new platform. The offering already has 15 paying customers, and the company is using the platform internally to refine features and accelerate learning, signaling early but tangible traction in the market.

AI Engagement Metrics Surge Across the Portfolio

Management highlighted sharp year-over-year increases in AI usage, pointing to strong adoption of its newer capabilities. AI skill benchmark completions jumped 994% while AI content and AI Journey completions rose 261% and 222% respectively, and CAISY users and launches climbed triple digits, underpinning confidence in AI as a key growth driver.

Margins Improve on Stronger Adjusted EBITDA

Adjusted EBITDA in the quarter reached $31.2 million, up about 4% from a year earlier, and the adjusted EBITDA margin widened to 23.9% from 22.4%. The TDS segment contributed roughly $33 million of EBITDA and drove most of the improvement, underscoring the segment’s role as the company’s profit engine.

Free Cash Flow Turns Positive and Leverage Eases

Free cash flow more than doubled year over year in the quarter to $26.5 million, helping full-year free cash flow turn modestly positive at around $6.5 million, slightly above expectations. Cash and equivalents stood at $104.5 million while net debt edged down to roughly $474 million, signaling incremental balance sheet progress.

Expense Efficiency Supports Growth Investments

Operating expenses fell meaningfully, with content and software development and selling and marketing each down around 5% and G&A lower by about 13% year over year. Total operating expenses declined 4.2%, or $4.3 million, enabling Skillsoft to reinvest savings into strategic growth areas without sacrificing profitability.

Guidance Highlights Profitable TDS Outlook

Looking ahead, Skillsoft guided TDS fiscal 2027 revenue to $388 million–$406 million and adjusted EBITDA to $108 million–$116 million, implying margins around 28%. The company also forecast TDS free cash flow of $14 million–$22 million, excluding Global Knowledge, signaling confidence in the segment’s cash generation despite modest top-line expectations.

Revenue Declines and Mixed Top-Line Trends

Total revenue for the quarter was $130.7 million, down 2.3% year over year, as growth remained elusive. TDS revenue of $102.6 million was essentially flat, while the Learner and Global Knowledge businesses failed to expand, reflecting lingering demand challenges in certain parts of the portfolio.

Global Knowledge Drags, Triggering Strategic Review

Global Knowledge revenue fell about 9.4% to $28 million, continuing its underperformance relative to the rest of the business. Management has launched a strategic review and potential divestiture of the unit, citing its weaker growth profile and a desire to focus capital and management attention on higher-priority assets.

GAAP Loss Widens as Adjusted Earnings Slip

GAAP net loss deepened to $36.7 million for the quarter, compared with $31.1 million a year ago, largely due to an intangible impairment and higher restructuring costs. Adjusted net income also retreated to $11 million, with adjusted EPS sliding from $2.11 to $1.26, highlighting the gap between underlying profitability and reported results.

Retention Solid but Upgrades Under Pressure

Skillsoft reported a TDS trailing twelve-month dollar retention rate of 98%, down from 105% in the prior-year period, as upgrade activity cooled. While customer retention improved, the decline in upsell reflected tougher comparisons and a cautious spending environment, limiting expansion revenue for now.

Higher Utilization Lifts Costs of Revenue

Cost of revenue increased 2.5% year over year to $34.2 million, or 26% of revenue, driven by higher labs and certification spending tied to greater customer utilization. Management is adjusting agreement structures to curb future cost overruns, suggesting some of the pressure is being addressed contractually.

Macro and Geopolitics Weigh on Bookings and Sales

Executives cited executive orders, government market disruptions and broader geopolitical instability as factors damping bookings in the quarter. They also noted that the Global Knowledge sale process has been hampered by conflict in the Middle East and limited buyer availability, adding uncertainty around the timing and outcome of any transaction.

Conservative Outlook Reflects Ongoing Consumer Weakness

The midpoint of TDS guidance implies a slight year-over-year revenue decline of about $7 million, driven almost entirely by the consumer-focused learner business, which represents roughly 9% of TDS. Management signaled that this segment is expected to continue shrinking, and that the overall outlook intentionally embeds a conservative stance amid persistent macro risks.

In sum, Skillsoft’s earnings call showcased a company leaning into AI and efficiency to offset revenue softness and macro uncertainty. Investors will be watching to see whether strong AI adoption, margin expansion and cash generation can eventually translate into more durable top-line growth, especially as management reshapes the portfolio and executes on its TDS-focused strategy.

data.title: NexTech AR Earnings Call Flags High-Margin Growth
data.description:
NexTech AR Solutions Corp ((TSE:NTAR)) has held its Q4 earnings call. Read on for the main highlights of the call.

Easter Sale - 70% Off TipRanks

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Discover top-performing stock ideas and upgrade to a portfolio of market leaders with Smart Investor Picks

NexTech AR Solutions Corp’s latest earnings call struck an upbeat tone, with management framing the quarter as an inflection point. Strong top line growth, record margins and new enterprise traction were weighed against cash discipline, deal dependencies and product rollout risks, but executives argued the upside now clearly outweighs the remaining headwinds.

Explosive Revenue Growth Marks a New Curve

NexTech reported 59% year‑over‑year revenue growth alongside roughly 20% sequential gains for the second straight quarter, which management called evidence of a new sustainable growth curve. They positioned this momentum as a break from the company’s past volatility, suggesting the business is now entering a more durable expansion phase.

Software Shift Drives Ultra‑High Margins

The company posted record gross margins of about 95%, a level more typical of pure‑play software platforms than services businesses. Management credited a pivot away from labor‑intensive 3D modeling toward software and AI‑enabled offerings, arguing this high‑margin mix provides a stronger foundation for long‑term profitability.

Big‑Name Enterprises Signal Rising Market Credibility

Executives highlighted a roster of marquee enterprise customers, including global tech platforms, automakers and financial institutions, as proof of NexTech’s growing relevance. They added that multiple new enterprise contracts are in final negotiation stages with signatures expected soon, potentially adding meaningful recurring revenue.

Eventdex and Krafty Deals Broaden Event Tech Stack

Recent acquisitions are central to NexTech’s expansion strategy, with Eventdex adding end‑to‑end event tools like ticketing, badging and AI matchmaking. The newer Krafty Labs deal brings virtual and experiential team‑building, gifting and off‑site experiences, opening incremental revenue streams across the broader events economy.

AI‑First Operating Model Targets Scale and Efficiency

The company is leaning heavily into an AI‑first strategy to streamline operations and support profitable growth across acquired units. Management said AI automation is already helping reduce headcount needs, improve margins and support faster customer scaling, and they see continued optimization ahead as more workflows are automated.

Blockchain Ticketing and Analytics Headline Product Roadmap

On the product front, NexTech plans to roll out blockchain‑enabled ticketing aimed at curbing counterfeits and enhancing authenticity and certification. In parallel, it is building an AI‑driven analytics platform for Krafty to deepen customer insights, with demos and product updates slated over the coming weeks.

Management Signals Conviction with Insider Buying

Management underscored confidence in the growth trajectory, reiterating an internal goal of triple‑digit revenue expansion and earlier‑than‑expected cash‑flow breakeven. The CEO highlighted a recent personal purchase of 550,000 shares at a depressed price level, framing it as a vote of confidence and hinting at potential additional buying.

High Conviction, But No Formal Guidance

Despite the bullish tone, NexTech stopped short of issuing formal revenue or earnings guidance for the year. Executives argued it remains early to commit to specific numbers given ongoing deals and product launches, even as they repeatedly emphasized their belief in triple‑digit growth potential.

ARway Deal Stalled by Audit and Regulatory Steps

The acquisition of ARway remains signed but not closed, with completion tied to audited financials and regulatory approvals still in progress. Management admitted they delayed a separate audit to preserve cash, which in turn has pushed out the closing timeline for this strategic transaction.

Ticketing Rollout Faces Adoption and Timing Uncertainties

While blockchain ticketing is positioned as a potential flagship product in a massive global market, management cautioned that adoption will not be instantaneous. Integrating with large established ticketing platforms and driving meaningful transaction volume will likely take time, leaving execution and timing risks on the table.

Legacy 3D Business Fades as Company Pivots

NexTech’s historical 3D modeling business, once driven by a sizable contract, has shrunk to what management now describes as only hundreds of thousands in annual revenue. This effectively marks an exit from a previous multimillion‑dollar line and underscores how fully the company is repositioning around software, AI and events.

Deal‑Driven Strategy Brings Integration Risk

The growth blueprint leans heavily on mergers and acquisitions to accelerate scale, customer wins and product depth. While management views M&A as a powerful turbocharger, they acknowledged that successful integration is not guaranteed, leaving execution risk if future deals underperform or fail to materialize.

Cash Discipline and Volatile Stock Shape Risk Profile

Management’s decision to delay certain audits to conserve cash, combined with a historically volatile and currently low share price, points to funding and market perception challenges. The CEO’s recent share purchase was framed as a counter‑signal to investor caution, but it does not fully remove financing and valuation concerns.

Directional Guidance Points to Aggressive Growth Ambitions

Looking ahead, NexTech outlined directional goals rather than hard targets, calling for triple‑digit revenue growth this year and cash‑flow positivity sooner than previously expected. They cited the current run‑rate, 95% margins, recent acquisitions, a sizable customer base and a large events and ticketing market as tailwinds, while planning to move smaller deals up into six‑figure tiers.

NexTech’s earnings call painted the picture of a company in strategic transition, trading a shrinking legacy 3D business for a software‑driven, AI‑powered events platform. Execution on acquisitions, blockchain ticketing and enterprise deals will be crucial, but for now the mix of rapid growth, elite customers and ultra‑high margins is reshaping the risk‑reward profile in the company’s favor.

data.title: Precipio Earnings Call Signals Profitable Growth Pivot
data.description:
Precipio ((PRPO)) has held its Q4 earnings call. Read on for the main highlights of the call.

Easter Sale - 70% Off TipRanks

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Discover top-performing stock ideas and upgrade to a portfolio of market leaders with Smart Investor Picks

Precipio’s latest earnings call painted a cautiously optimistic picture, as management highlighted a move to positive cash flow, 30% revenue growth to $24M, and better gross margins alongside a cleaner balance sheet. Executives also acknowledged uneven performance in the Products division and execution risks, but outlined concrete fixes and a clear strategy aimed at accelerating growth into 2026.

Revenue Growth and Scale

Precipio reported fiscal 2025 revenue of $24.0M, up 30% year over year, underscoring solid demand momentum despite pockets of volatility. Growth was led by Pathology Services, where increased demand for specialized diagnostic and molecular testing continued to pull in higher case volumes from both new and existing customers.

Positive Cash Flow and Balance Sheet Cleanup

Management emphasized reaching a strategic inflection point as the company turned positive on cash flow during 2025, easing prior funding concerns. The repayment of the Change Healthcare loan and the exercise of remaining financial warrants simplified the capital structure, giving the company more flexibility to reinvest in growth.

Gross Margin Improvement

Consolidated gross margin improved from 41% in 2024 to 45% in 2025, reflecting better economics as volume scaled. Executives attributed this 4‑point gain primarily to higher case volumes, a more favorable case mix, and operational efficiencies within Pathology Services that reduced unit costs.

Pathology Services: Organic Growth and Leverage

Pathology Services continued to be the growth engine, benefiting from both new customer wins and increased testing from existing accounts. With laboratory infrastructure still operating below capacity, management noted that each additional case drops through at higher incremental margin, strengthening both profitability and cash generation.

Products Manufacturing Scalability

The Products division showcased its manufacturing scalability in Q4, posting an eye‑catching product gross margin of 90% versus about 30% in Q3 and a historical 40–50% range. This spike came from a concentrated production run to prebuild inventory for expected Q1 downtime, illustrating that higher volume can support a much more attractive margin profile.

Commercial Team Strengthened

Recognizing that sales coverage had been a bottleneck, Precipio significantly upgraded its commercial team in January 2026 with the hiring of a seasoned Chief Commercial Officer and two experienced business development professionals. This marks a step up from a largely part‑time, under‑resourced effort and is intended to accelerate adoption of the company’s products.

Clinical Validation and Thought Leadership

Management spotlighted new clinical and academic validation as a key competitive asset, including a joint study with Memorial Sloan Kettering supporting the Bloodhound BCR‑ABL platform. A poster presentation with Wayne State University on the HemeScreen panel further reinforced Precipio’s presence in the molecular diagnostics community and bolstered its scientific credibility.

Market Engagement and Share Price Momentum

The company reported more than 50 investor interactions during 2025 and pointed to roughly 300% share‑price appreciation over the year as a sign of growing market confidence. Management framed this performance as a byproduct of both improved fundamentals and increased outreach to the investment community.

Strategic Product-Service Opportunities

A centerpiece of the call was a plan to launch a next‑day rapid molecular AML testing service that blends the strengths of Products and Pathology Services. Targeting an estimated $500M U.S. market, the offering aims to deliver next‑day results with a comprehensive five‑day follow‑up report, shortening turnaround times for a critical oncology indication.

Products Revenue Underperformance

Despite strategic promise, the Products division fell short of revenue expectations in 2025 as operational pauses and customer‑side disruptions offset new business wins. Machine downtime, staffing gaps, and onboarding delays at key labs muted the topline contribution from products relative to management’s initial plans.

Earlier Commercial Resource Constraints

Management was candid that the Products business operated for much of 2025 with an undersized commercial footprint, relying on one senior executive part‑time plus a junior sales representative. This lean structure limited customer acquisition and slowed pipeline conversion, a gap the January 2026 hiring spree is designed to close.

Product Margin Volatility and One-Time Effects

Product margins were highly volatile, swinging from roughly 30% in Q3 to 90% in Q4 as the company invested in expanded facilities and then executed a concentrated production run. Executives stressed that the fourth‑quarter margin is not a new baseline but rather a demonstration of potential profitability should volume remain consistently elevated.

Customer Onboarding and Sales Cycle Delays

The call underscored that diagnostic industry adoption timelines can be slow, with customers needing to validate assays and adjust workflows, IT systems, and regulatory processes before going live. These multi‑step onboarding requirements delayed revenue recognition and contributed to the slower‑than‑expected ramp in product revenues.

Operational Dependence and Revenue Fluctuation Risk

Precipio’s revenue remains partly exposed to operational interruptions at customer labs, including equipment downtime and staffing shortages that can temporarily halt testing. Management highlighted a strategy to act as a backup lab for clients to mitigate some of this dependence, but acknowledged that these factors created noticeable revenue variability in 2025.

Planned Manufacturing Downtime and Inventory Timing

The company prebuilt product inventory in Q4 to cover an anticipated two‑to‑four‑week equipment downtime in Q1 2026, which drove the one‑time margin distortion. While the move protected near‑term customer supply, it also emphasized how production scheduling and transitions can temporarily skew financial metrics and must be managed carefully.

Forward-Looking Guidance and Strategic Mix Shift

Looking ahead, management plans to reinvest cash from the higher‑margin pathology business into scaling the Products segment, pursuing a sizable U.S. opportunity. Over the next three to five years, the goal is to transition from a roughly 90/10 services‑to‑products revenue mix toward a more balanced portfolio while building on the new commercial hires and the AML testing launch.

Precipio’s earnings call portrayed a company turning an important financial corner while still wrestling with the growing pains of scaling a products business. For investors, the key watch points will be execution on the expanded commercial strategy, stabilization of product margins, and progress toward a more diversified revenue mix without sacrificing the momentum in Pathology Services.

data.title: Phoenix Education’s Earnings Call Balances Growth and Headwinds
data.description:
Phoenix Education Partners, Inc. ((PXED)) has held its Q2 earnings call. Read on for the main highlights of the call.

Easter Sale - 70% Off TipRanks

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Discover top-performing stock ideas and upgrade to a portfolio of market leaders with Smart Investor Picks

Phoenix Education Partners, Inc. struck a cautiously upbeat tone on its latest earnings call, highlighting resilient operations despite near-term revenue pressure and higher public-company costs. Management emphasized stronger student retention, expanding margins and a fortified balance sheet, arguing these fundamentals outweigh modest top-line slippage and the hit to GAAP earnings from share-based compensation.

Enrollment Growth and Stronger Student Retention

Average degreed enrollment ticked up 1.8% in Q2 to about 82,600 students, with first-half enrollment up 2.9% to roughly 84,100. Retention from the most recent annual cohort improved to 76.6%, an increase of about 500 basis points year over year, boosting enrollment quality and helping reduce bad debt expense.

Adjusted EBITDA and Margin Expansion

Adjusted EBITDA rose 7.8% in Q2 to $34.8 million and climbed 7.4% in the first half to $110.0 million, underscoring improving profitability. The adjusted EBITDA margin expanded to 15.7% in Q2 from 14.5% and to 22.7% for the first six months, up 130 basis points as cost discipline and efficiencies kicked in.

Balance Sheet Strength with Solid Cash and No Debt

Phoenix ended the quarter with about $252.1 million in cash, cash equivalents and marketable securities, up from $194.8 million at the prior fiscal year-end. With no outstanding debt and roughly $80 million in operating cash generated year to date, the company underscored its financial flexibility to invest and return capital.

Momentum in Employer-Affiliated B2B Channel

Employer-affiliated students represented 35% of total enrollment in Q2, up from 31% a year earlier, now accounting for more than one-third of the student base. Management highlighted that these B2B learners typically show higher retention and durability than direct-to-consumer students, supporting longer-term revenue stability.

Technology and AI Investments Driving Efficiency

The company reported meaningful progress in deploying AI tools across the university, including AI-assisted onboarding, 24/7 support and better adviser productivity. Enhanced transcript evaluation and automation are contributing to margin expansion and improved retention, as technology reduces administrative friction for both staff and students.

Student Satisfaction and Industry-Leading Skill Credentials

Phoenix said it outperformed national benchmarks on the Ruffalo Noel Levitz online learner survey, with 85% of students very satisfied or satisfied versus a 73% national average. The university has issued more than 1 million employer-informed digital skill badges, and 79% of surveyed students reported confidence using AI tools in real-world settings.

Capital Returns and Evolving Capital Allocation Strategy

The board authorized a $50 million share repurchase program alongside a quarterly dividend of $0.21 per share, targeting about $0.84 annually subject to approval. Management reiterated its capital priorities of investing in students and technology, maintaining a strong balance sheet and returning capital to shareholders where appropriate.

Q2 Net Revenue Dip Despite Operational Strength

Net revenue in Q2 slipped 0.4% year over year to $222.5 million, reflecting discounts and near-term marketing dynamics. This modest decline contrasts with the company’s improving EBITDA profile, highlighting the gap between accounting revenue trends and underlying operating momentum.

Net Income Hit by Higher Share-Based Compensation

GAAP net income attributable to Phoenix fell to $10.8 million, or $0.28 per diluted share, compared with $16.1 million or $0.43 a year earlier. For the first six months, net income dropped about 58.1% to $26.2 million, driven largely by higher share-based compensation tied to the IPO and other public-company expenses.

Marketing Funnel Disruption from Search and AI Changes

Management cited changes to major search algorithms, including AI-driven shifts that favored video platforms, as a headwind to the marketing funnel and the key January enrollment season. The company has been migrating content toward YouTube and reported early signs of improvement in March, but expects timing effects to echo into Q3 and Q4.

Revenue Guidance Skewed to Lower End on Mix and Discounts

Executives cautioned that revenue growth may lag enrollment as discounts and a growing B2B mix weigh on average revenue per student. Since employer-affiliated enrollments are typically discounted, the shift toward B2B can damp near-term revenue visibility even as total enrollment and retention improve.

Managing Fraud Prevention Friction

Phoenix noted that enhanced fraud detection and verification measures had previously introduced friction into the enrollment process, affecting prospective students. Improved analytics have reduced unnecessary hurdles for legitimate applicants, though some incremental friction remains and will require ongoing monitoring and refinement.

Higher Public-Company Costs Pressuring GAAP Earnings

While adjusted EBITDA benefited from cost controls and efficiency gains, public-company costs and share-based compensation significantly pressured GAAP net income. Management framed these expenses as a structural step-up now embedded in the cost base, which investors will need to weigh against the healthier cash flows and margins.

Forward-Looking Guidance and Outlook

Phoenix reiterated its fiscal 2026 guidance for net revenue of $1.025 billion to $1.035 billion and adjusted EBITDA of $244 million to $249 million, with revenue expected near the low end and EBITDA toward the high end. The company is banking on disciplined cost management, lower bad debt, AI-driven efficiencies and B2B growth to offset marketing disruptions and support margin expansion.

Phoenix Education’s latest call painted a picture of a business trading some near-term revenue and earnings volatility for better quality growth and stronger economics. For investors, the story now hinges on whether marketing funnel repairs, AI initiatives and the B2B push can sustain enrollment and margins while GAAP profitability gradually catches up to the company’s cash-generating power.

Trio Petroleum Corp. ( (TPET) ) just unveiled an announcement.

Trio Petroleum Corp. has been actively updating its at-the-market equity offering, which was originally launched under a sales agreement with Ladenburg Thalmann & Co. Inc. and supported by a Form S-3 registration statement effective September 10, 2024. Beginning January 9, 2026, the company filed a series of prospectus supplement amendments with the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission to adjust the amount of common stock eligible for sale under regulatory limits.

Between March 3 and April 8, 2026, Trio filed Amendments No. 1 through No. 8, progressively increasing the maximum aggregate offering amount from $6.89 million to $22.93 million while disclosing cumulative sales of approximately 23.63 million shares for $21.87 million. Following the latest amendment on April 8, 2026, the company reported that $1.06 million of additional common stock remained available for sale, signaling continued reliance on this at-the-market program to bolster liquidity and support ongoing corporate funding needs, with the legal framework confirmed by external counsel.

More about Trio Petroleum Corp.

Trio Petroleum Corp. is a Delaware-incorporated company that issues and trades common stock as part of its capital-raising activities in the public markets. The company utilizes at-the-market equity programs through a sales agent to periodically sell registered shares to investors, indicating a financing strategy focused on incremental issuance rather than large, single equity offerings.
